title: Choose the Brain (LLM) for Your Agent
lede: The model you pick is your agent's brain — it decides how smart, how fast, and how natural every conversation feels. This guide walks you through the Brains (LLM) picker, realtime vs. text models, temperature, backup models, and the Transcriber (STT) that turns callers' speech into text.

steps.title: Open Your Agent
steps.content:
Every model setting lives inside an individual agent, so start by opening one:

  1. In the left sidebar, click Intelligence Center → Smart Agents.
  2. Look at the list: the LLM, STT, and TTS columns show at a glance which brain, transcriber, and voice each agent uses.
  3. Click the name of the agent you want to configure. The agent editor opens.

images.alt: Smart Agents list with LLM, STT, and TTS columns
images.caption: The Smart Agents list — the LLM column shows each agent's current brain.

images.alt: Smart Agents list with LLM, STT, and TTS columns
images.caption: The Smart Agents list — the LLM column shows each agent's current brain.

steps.title: Find the Three Engine Panels
steps.content:
In the editor's middle column, three panels together power every conversation:

  • Transcriber (STT) — turns the caller's speech into text (speech-to-text).
  • Brains (LLM) — the language model that reads the conversation and decides what to say.
  • Voice (TTS) — speaks the reply out loud (text-to-speech).

Each row shows a green pill with the current provider and model (for example google / gemini-3.1-flash-lite), a gray Backup: note if a fallback is configured, and an ON/OFF switch.

⚠️ Voice, Transcriber, and LLM are critical for voice calls — leave their switches ON. If one is off, its status pill in the page header turns red and the agent can't handle calls properly.

images.alt: Agent editor with Transcriber, Voice, and Brains panels in the middle column
images.caption: The agent editor — Transcriber (STT), Voice (TTS), and Brains (LLM) sit in the middle column.

images.alt: Agent editor with Transcriber, Voice, and Brains panels in the middle column
images.caption: The agent editor — Transcriber (STT), Voice (TTS), and Brains (LLM) sit in the middle column.

steps.title: Open the Brains (LLM) Picker
steps.content:
1. Click the Brains (LLM) row. The model picker opens.
  2. On the left rail you'll see the provider groups available on your account — for example Realtime Agent, Google (Gemini family), OpenAI (GPT family), and Anthropic (Claude family). Each group lists its model count and a one-line specialty.
  3. Click a provider group to browse its models in the Model grid on the right. Browsing groups never changes anything — only clicking a model card does.
  4. When you're finished, click Done to close the picker.
💡 Every pick saves instantly — there is no separate Save button. Done simply closes the window.

images.alt: Brains (LLM) picker with provider groups on the left and model grid on the right
images.caption: The Brains (LLM) picker — provider groups on the left, model cards and Temperature on the right.

images.alt: Brains (LLM) picker with provider groups on the left and model grid on the right
images.caption: The Brains (LLM) picker — provider groups on the left, model cards and Temperature on the right.

steps.title: Realtime vs. Text Models — Pick the Right Type
steps.content:
The picker offers two fundamentally different kinds of brain:

  • Realtime models (the Realtime Agent group — e.g. gpt-realtime-1.5 or gemini-2.5-flash-native-audio): one single model listens to audio and speaks back directly (speech-to-speech). Transcription and turn detection are built in, so the Transcriber (STT) panel disappears from the editor. Best for: the most natural, lowest-latency conversations.
  • Text (LLM) models (every other group): the classic pipeline — Speech → Transcriber (STT) → LLM → Voice (TTS). You choose each stage yourself, including any TTS voice on the platform. Best for: full control, voice cloning, wider model choice, and predictable cost.
💡 Picking a Google realtime model automatically switches your agent to a compatible built-in Google voice — and switching back to a text model restores your previous voice. iMash handles the swap for you.

images.alt: Realtime Agent group showing speech-to-speech models
images.caption: The Realtime Agent group — single-stream speech-to-speech models.

images.alt: Realtime Agent group showing speech-to-speech models
images.caption: The Realtime Agent group — single-stream speech-to-speech models.

steps.title: Select a Model
steps.content:
1. Click the provider group you want on the left rail.
  2. Click a model card in the grid. The card highlights with a blue check mark, and the change saves immediately.
  3. Close the picker with Done — the Brains (LLM) row now shows your new pick in its green pill.

Quick guidance on choosing:

  • Fast + affordable (high call volume): light models like gemini-3.1-flash-lite or gpt-5-mini.
  • Strong reasoning (complex scripts, many tools): larger models like gpt-5 or the Anthropic Claude models.
  • Most human feel: a Realtime Agent model.

images.alt: Engine panels showing the selected provider and model pills
images.caption: After picking, the panel pills reflect your provider and model choices.

images.alt: Engine panels showing the selected provider and model pills
images.caption: After picking, the panel pills reflect your provider and model choices.

steps.title: Tune the Temperature
steps.content:
Below the model grid sits the Temperature slider (0 to 2). It controls how adventurous the model's replies are:

  • Lower (0 – 0.4) — deterministic and on-script. Ideal for support agents, dispatchers, and anything that must follow rules precisely.
  • Middle (0.4 – 0.8) — the sweet spot for most voice agents: natural variety without going off the rails.
  • Higher (0.8 – 2) — creative and unpredictable. Rarely what you want on a phone call.
  1. Drag the slider to your value — it saves as you release it.

images.alt: Temperature slider in the Brains picker
images.caption: Temperature: lower = more deterministic, higher = more creative.

images.alt: Temperature slider in the Brains picker
images.caption: Temperature: lower = more deterministic, higher = more creative.

steps.title: Set a Backup Model (Recommended)
steps.content:
Providers occasionally have outages. A backup model keeps your agent talking when the primary fails:

  1. In the Brains (LLM) picker header, switch the toggle from Production to Backup. A yellow banner confirms: "You're configuring the Backup LLM."
  2. Pick a provider and model exactly as before — but choose a different provider than your production model, so one provider's outage can't take both down.
  3. Switch back to Production and click Done.

During calls, the agent always uses the Production model first and only fails over to the Backup if the primary errors or times out. The panel row shows a gray Backup: note once one is set.

💡 The same Production/Backup toggle exists in the Transcriber (STT) and Voice (TTS) pickers — set all three for a fully failover-proof agent.

images.alt: Backup LLM view with warning banner and model selection
images.caption: Backup view — picks here only apply when the production model fails.

images.alt: Backup LLM view with warning banner and model selection
images.caption: Backup view — picks here only apply when the production model fails.

steps.title: Configure the Transcriber (Text Models Only)
steps.content:
If you chose a text LLM, the agent needs a speech-to-text engine to hear the caller. Click the Transcriber (STT) row to open its picker:

  1. Choose a provider on the left rail. Each card shows its specialty and a live latency chip (lower is snappier):
    • Deepgram — multi-language, configurable model, low latency. The default and best all-rounder.
    • Speechmatics — real-time with Speaker Diarization (a toggle that separates speakers in the transcript); requires an explicit language.
    • AssemblyAI — English-only transcription.
    • Groq — fast inference; requires an explicit ISO-639-1 language code (e.g. en, es).
  2. Set the Language the caller will speak — pick Multilingual (Deepgram) if calls arrive in mixed languages, or a specific language for the best accuracy.
  3. With Deepgram, also pick the Model (e.g. Flux (Multilingual) or a Nova model) and optionally add Keyword Boost terms — domain-specific words like product names or model numbers that the transcriber should recognize more reliably.
  4. Click Done when finished.
⚠️ Using a Realtime Agent model? The Transcriber panel is hidden — realtime models transcribe on their own, so there's nothing to configure here.

images.alt: Transcriber Provider picker with provider list and settings
images.caption: The Transcriber Provider picker — providers on the left, language and model settings on the right.

images.alt: Transcriber Provider picker with provider list and settings
images.caption: The Transcriber Provider picker — providers on the left, language and model settings on the right.

images.alt: Deepgram transcriber settings: language, model, and keyword boost
images.caption: Deepgram settings — Language, Model, and Keyword Boost for domain-specific terms.

images.alt: Deepgram transcriber settings: language, model, and keyword boost
images.caption: Deepgram settings — Language, Model, and Keyword Boost for domain-specific terms.

steps.title: Test Your New Brain
steps.content:
Models feel different in conversation — a spec sheet never tells the whole story. Compare them live:

  1. Click Connect in the test panel on the right side of the editor (or Test Agent in the header) and have a short conversation.
  2. Swap the model in Brains (LLM), reconnect, and ask the same questions again.
  3. Judge latency, tone, and how well the agent follows your prompt — then keep the winner.

That's it — your agent now thinks with exactly the brain you chose, with a safety net behind it. 🚀

💡 Working in the Node view instead? The LLM and Transcriber canvas nodes open these same pickers — everything in this guide applies there too.

support.title: Need Help?
support.content:
If you run into any issues or have questions:

  • 📧 Email us at support@imash.io
  • 💬 Join our Discord community
  • 📚 Check out our API Reference
  • 🎥 Watch our video tutorials
